title: MyLifeBits: A Personal Database for Everything
research_question: How can a database platform capture, organize, and retrieve the heterogeneous digital material accumulated across a person's lifetime?
method: Five-year system report describing the SQL-based MyLifeBits platform, its capture and annotation tools, and experience using it as a growing personal lifetime store.
population_or_data: Gordon Bell's digitized archive and ongoing personal data, including documents, email, web pages, photos, audio, video, calls, meetings, interaction logs, and SenseCam images.
core_finding: The project expanded from storing scanned and born-digital files into a unified, searchable store for continuously captured multimodal personal material, showing the feasibility of a database-centered lifetime archive but not establishing that capture alone produces reliable autobiographical recall.
architecture_implications: Keep raw artifacts and typed metadata in a durable personal event store instead of reducing all history to prose summaries.
Support cross-modal search, links, annotations, and multiple views because no single folder hierarchy fits lifetime data.
Treat format migration, provenance, and selective deletion as first-class lifecycle operations for a long-lived brain.
limitations: This is a system and use-experience report centered largely on one technically sophisticated participant; it provides no controlled fidelity or retrieval evaluation, and continuous capture raises unresolved privacy, bystander-consent, curation, and long-term format-access problems.

search_queries: site:microsoft.com MyLifeBits a personal database for everything Gemmell Bell Lueder
site:dl.acm.org 10.1145/1107458.1107460 MyLifeBits

title: SenseCam: A Retrospective Memory Aid
research_question: Can an automatically triggered wearable camera and sensor log provide effective cues for retrospective autobiographical recall?
method: SenseCam device design plus an initial 12-month clinical trial in which a patient with amnesia periodically reviewed automatically captured event images.
population_or_data: One patient with amnesia wearing a sensor-augmented still camera that recorded daily images and sensor readings over a year.
core_finding: Periodic review of SenseCam event images enabled the participant to recall recorded experiences that had previously been inaccessible, providing early evidence that passive visual cues can support retrospective memory.
architecture_implications: Segment passive capture into reviewable events rather than exposing an undifferentiated image stream.
Preserve capture time and sensor context alongside each cue so retrieval can reconstruct an episode.
Design memory review as an explicit user-controlled operation; storage without rehearsal is not the demonstrated intervention.
limitations: The reported evaluation is a single-patient clinical trial, so efficacy cannot be generalized to healthy users or other impairments; the abstract does not establish long-term independence from repeated review, and wearable capture creates substantial bystander and intimacy risks.

search_queries: site:link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/11853565_11 SenseCam retrospective memory aid
SenseCam A Retrospective Memory Aid DOI 2006

title: Do Life-Logging Technologies Support Memory for the Past? An Experimental Study Using SenseCam
research_question: How do automatically captured SenseCam images and deliberately captured photos differ as cues for remembering and knowing past personal events over time?
method: Experimental study of everyday event capture and later memory judgments, comparing automatic SenseCam images with user-captured images and distinguishing recollection from familiarity.
population_or_data: Participants' everyday experiences represented by automatically and deliberately captured image sets and tested at multiple delays.
core_finding: SenseCam images helped participants reconnect with past events, but their contribution changed over time and differed between vivid remembering and a sense of knowing; automatically captured and user-selected images were not interchangeable memory cues.
architecture_implications: Represent confidence and memory type separately instead of treating every retrieved artifact as equivalent evidence of recollection.
Retain both passively captured context and user-selected salience signals because they support different retrieval functions.
Evaluate retrieval at multiple delays, since cue utility can change as biological memory decays.
limitations: The study evaluates image-cued memory under a bounded protocol rather than a complete lifetime archive; image presence is not proof that an interpretation is correct, and the abstract-level evidence does not establish transfer to text-only logs or long-term autonomous agents.

search_queries: site:dl.acm.org 10.1145/1240624.1240636 life-logging SenseCam
site:microsoft.com Do life-logging technologies support memory for the past SenseCam

title: Navigating Connected Memories with a Task-oriented Dialog System
research_question: Can a task-oriented dialogue system answer natural requests that navigate a user's connected, multimodal personal memories?
method: Constructed COMET with a two-stage pipeline: simulated dialogues over synthetic memory graphs followed by human paraphrasing, then benchmarked API prediction, multimodal coreference, dialogue-state tracking, and response generation.
population_or_data: About 11,500 dialogues and 103,000 utterances grounded in roughly 1,100 simulated memory graphs containing people, place, time, and media relations.
core_finding: COMET operationalizes personal-memory navigation as linked dialogue tasks and demonstrates that people, place, time, and image references must be resolved jointly rather than by plain semantic search alone.
architecture_implications: Maintain an entity-event graph beside embeddings so a query can traverse people, locations, times, and media relations.
Separate reference resolution, dialogue state, retrieval/API execution, and response generation so failures remain diagnosable.
Use privacy-preserving synthetic graphs for early testing, but require a distinct validation layer before claiming performance on real memories.
limitations: The memory graphs are simulated because real memories are private, and human work paraphrases rather than supplies authentic histories; the ontology and tasks are constrained, so results do not establish robustness to messy, contradictory, or evolving personal records.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org Navigating Connected Memories with a Task-oriented Dialog System COMET
2022.emnlp-main.160 connected memories

title: Social Simulacra: Creating Populated Prototypes for Social Computing Systems
research_question: Can language models populate social-computing prototypes with plausible users and interactions useful for design exploration?
method: Built a system that generates community members and interactions from goals, rules, and seed personas; evaluated outputs against 50 post-GPT-3 subreddits and conducted a design study with 16 social-computing designers.
population_or_data: Generated communities and interactions compared with content from 50 Reddit communities, plus feedback from 16 designers.
core_finding: Generated communities were plausible enough that evaluators misclassified generated versus real content at an average rate of 41%, and designers found populated prototypes useful for exploring possible behavior, while the authors explicitly reject treating them as point predictions of a real community.
architecture_implications: Use simulated populations to enumerate scenarios and stress cases, not to claim what a specific person will do.
Condition simulations on explicit community rules and diverse personas, and expose those assumptions with every generated trace.
Pair synthetic prototypes with participatory evaluation by the people represented before deployment decisions.
limitations: The technical evaluation is limited to English Reddit communities and plausibility can conceal model bias; a 16-person designer study does not establish downstream validity, generated users can be misused as fake participation, and simulation cannot replace engagement with real communities.

search_queries: site:dl.acm.org Social Simulacra Creating Populated Prototypes DOI
site:arxiv.org/abs 2208.04024 Social Simulacra

title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
research_question: Which memory and planning mechanisms let language-model agents produce believable individual and emergent social behavior over time?
method: Deployed 25 agents in a sandbox town with a scored memory stream, reflection, and planning; used ablations and a 100-participant human evaluation of agent interview responses.
population_or_data: Twenty-five simulated agents interacting over two in-world days, with interview comparisons ranked by 100 human evaluators.
core_finding: The complete memory-reflection-planning architecture produced more believable responses than ablated variants, and local memories could propagate into coordinated social behavior; observed failures included missed retrieval, fabricated embellishment of memories, and overly formal speech.
architecture_implications: Store an append-only observation stream and rank retrieval by relevance, recency, and salience rather than relevance alone.
Consolidate repeated observations into higher-level reflections while retaining links back to source memories.
Keep plans revisable as new observations arrive, and log which memories and reflections influenced each action.
limitations: The world contains only 25 agents over two game days; crowdworker believability is not ground truth about a real person, the simulation and interviews are text-mediated, costs were substantial, and the architecture still hallucinated or failed when retrieval omitted key evidence.

search_queries: site:dl.acm.org Generative Agents Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior DOI
site:arxiv.org/abs 2304.03442 generative agents

title: LLM Agents Grounded in Self-Reports Enable General-Purpose Simulation of Individuals
research_question: How do interview-, survey-, and combined self-report-grounded language-model agents compare in reproducing individuals' survey, personality, economic-game, and experimental responses?
method: Collected two-hour voice interviews and standardized survey responses from a stratified US sample; built interview-only, survey-only, and combined agents; and evaluated General Social Survey items, Big Five traits, five incentivized economic games, and five experiments against two-week retests and demographic and persona baselines.
population_or_data: 1,052 US participants with interview transcripts, survey and personality responses, five economic games, five experimental tasks, and two-week retest measurements.
core_finding: On General Social Survey items, interview-only, survey-only, and combined agents reached 83%, 82%, and 86% normalized accuracy, respectively, versus 74% for demographic agents; combined self-reports helped modestly, but performance varied by outcome and does not establish a complete or stable digital self.
architecture_implications: Preserve source and modality labels because rich interviews and structured surveys contribute overlapping but not identical evidence.
Report performance relative to each measure's human test-retest ceiling, by task family, and by subgroup rather than only as one aggregate score.
Bind any reusable personal-agent artifact to explicit access, withdrawal, and deletion controls because detailed self-reports create privacy and deanonymization risks.
limitations: The agents predict self-reported and experimental responses rather than natural real-world behavior; economic-game results did not differ significantly among agent types, the five-experiment aggregate analysis was underpowered, sample-level correlation structure was not reproduced, only OpenAI model families were tested, and the English-language US sample differed from population targets on several characteristics. Detailed self-reports also create privacy, deanonymization, and consent-withdrawal risks.

search_queries: site:arxiv.org/abs 2411.10109 LLM Agents Grounded in Self-Reports
arxiv 2411.10109 interview survey combined agents 1052 participants

title: Out of One, Many: Using Language Models to Simulate Human Samples
research_question: Can a language model conditioned on human demographic and sociopolitical backstories reproduce distributions and relationships observed in survey samples?
method: Ran three studies prompting GPT-3 with thousands of US survey-derived backstories and compared generated political responses with subgroup distributions and known associations in human data.
population_or_data: Thousands of respondent profiles and political-opinion measures from US survey datasets, including American National Election Study-style backstories.
core_finding: Conditioned model outputs reproduced several aggregate subgroup patterns and associations in US public-opinion data, but the authors do not claim that a generated response corresponds to the specific person whose profile conditioned it.
architecture_implications: Separate cohort-level simulation from individual-level prediction in system labels, metrics, and product claims.
Represent conditioning variables and sampling temperature as part of provenance because they materially shape simulated populations.
Validate associations and distributions against held-out human data before using simulations for scenario analysis.
limitations: The work is bounded to US politics and public opinion, uses GPT-3 and its training snapshot, is sensitive to prompts and sampling, may reflect training-data stereotypes, and explicitly lacks evidence of person-specific correspondence.

search_queries: site:cambridge.org/core Out of One Many Using Language Models to Simulate Human Samples
site:arxiv.org/abs 2209.06899 human samples

title: Using Large Language Models to Simulate Multiple Humans and Replicate Human Subject Studies
research_question: Can prompted language models simulate groups of human participants closely enough to replicate classic experimental effects?
method: Ran Turing Experiments with several GPT model variants on the Ultimatum Game, Garden Path sentences, the Milgram shock experiment, and Wisdom of Crowds, comparing simulated responses with published human findings.
population_or_data: Prompted model participants and published human results from four established behavioral experiments.
core_finding: Models reproduced major patterns in the first three experiments, but failed as human simulators in the Wisdom of Crowds task because they supplied implausibly accurate obscure facts, demonstrating that apparent behavioral replication can coexist with model-specific nonhuman capabilities.
architecture_implications: Include adversarial tasks where pretrained knowledge should differ from the target person's knowledge.
Track whether an answer is supported by personal evidence or by the base model's general knowledge.
Use multiple models and prompt variants to estimate simulator sensitivity rather than treating one generation as a person.
limitations: Only four relatively simple studies were tested; demographic conditioning was shallow, results are prompt- and model-dependent, training contamination cannot be ruled out, and successful aggregate replication does not establish individual fidelity.

search_queries: site:proceedings.mlr.press/v202 Aher simulate multiple humans replicate human subject studies
site:arxiv.org/abs 2208.10264 Turing Experiments

title: Twin-2K-500: A Dataset for Building Digital Twins of Over 2,000 People Based on Their Answers to Over 500 Questions
research_question: How well can per-person agents built from broad survey histories predict held-out answers and reproduce behavioral-experiment effects?
method: Collected more than 500 questions over four waves from a representative US panel, built individual language-model twins from each person's observed answers, and compared held-out accuracy and experimental-effect replication with human test-retest reliability.
population_or_data: 2,058 US participants, roughly 2.42 hours of responses per person across four waves, with 500-plus questions and held-out evaluation items.
core_finding: The reported twins averaged 71.72% held-out accuracy, or 87.67% of the 81.72% human test-retest level, yet reproduced only six of ten between-subject and two of five within-subject effects and showed systematic normative-answer failures.
architecture_implications: Collect repeated, broad measurements and reserve temporally later questions for genuine person-level holdouts.
Evaluate exact individual answers separately from aggregate causal effects because one can look strong while the other fails.
Detect base-model normative priors, especially when a twin answers what is generally correct rather than what its person chose.
limitations: This is a preprint using a US social-science panel and an initial proprietary-model pipeline; survey responses are not natural behavior, several experimental effects failed, and observed errors include overknowledge, reversed sunk-cost behavior, medical overtrust, and political divergence.

search_queries: site:arxiv.org/abs Twin-2K-500 digital twins 2000 people 500 questions
arxiv 2505.17479 test retest digital twins

title: How Far are LLMs from Being Our Digital Twins? A Benchmark for Persona-Based Behavior Chain Simulation
research_question: Can persona-conditioned language models maintain fidelity across a temporally dependent chain of a person's behaviors rather than answer isolated questions?
method: Introduced BehaviorChain with multiple-choice and generation tasks, evaluated ten language models on sequential behavior nodes, and measured both per-node and cumulative chain accuracy.
population_or_data: 15,846 behaviors arranged into 10-20-node chains for 1,001 personas derived from literature and biographies.
core_finding: The best evaluated models remained below about 0.62 average score and 0.21 cumulative chain score; early mistakes propagated, recent behavior history mattered, and models performed worse on less salient persona behaviors.
architecture_implications: Evaluate complete trajectories and cumulative consistency, not only independent next-answer accuracy.
Give recent observed behavior explicit weight while preserving stable persona evidence and their provenance.
Support branch correction after a bad prediction so one hallucinated action does not become false history.
limitations: Personas and chains come largely from English-language Western literature and biographies rather than longitudinal logs of consenting users; source extraction is partly synthetic, generation scoring relies on an LLM judge, and human evaluation covers only a subset.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org 2025.findings-acl.813 digital twins BehaviorChain
How Far are LLMs from Being Our Digital Twins behavior chain benchmark

title: TwinVoice: A Multi-dimensional Benchmark Towards Digital Twins via LLM Persona Simulation
research_question: How well do language models simulate persona knowledge, behavior, memory, and style across public social, archived interpersonal-dialogue, and fictional narrative contexts?
method: Built discriminative and generative tasks over six capabilities in three persona dimensions and evaluated frontier and open language models with automatic scoring and limited human validation.
population_or_data: 2,000 social-persona samples of 15 turns, 2,500 interpersonal samples of 30 turns, and 1,187 narrative-persona samples.
core_finding: Even leading models showed a large gap between discriminative recognition and generative simulation: top discrimination reached 76.2, while the best aggregate generative score was 48.5, with persona tone and memory recall among the weakest abilities.
architecture_implications: Test a personal model across public voice, relationship-conditioned dialogue, and longitudinal narrative rather than collapsing them into one persona prompt.
Separate recognition probes from free generation because a model may identify the right trait without producing behavior consistent with it.
Evaluate relationship- and channel-conditioned recall separately from global facts before assuming that behavior transfers across contexts.
limitations: The social corpus is public Chinese microblog data, the interpersonal dimension comes from a public Pushshift Telegram archive rather than consented private conversations, and the narrative personas are fictional; the contexts do not form one person's integrated life, language coverage differs by dimension, and much generative evaluation depends on LLM judges with human validation limited to a small subset.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org 2026.findings-acl.981 TwinVoice digital twins
TwinVoice multi-dimensional benchmark persona simulation

title: Can LLM Agents Simulate Multi-Turn Human Behavior? Evidence from Real Online Customer Behavior Data
research_question: Can language-model agents predict the next action in real, multi-turn online shopping sessions at the individual-session level?
method: Introduced ShopCART from consented beta-shopping logs, compared prompted proprietary and open models, and fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B with synthetic reasoning traces for next-action prediction.
population_or_data: 31,865 sessions from 3,526 users, containing 230,965 actions and 4,432 purchases.
core_finding: Prompted models achieved at most 11.86% exact next-action accuracy; a fine-tuned model reached 17.26% accuracy and 33.86% F1, while prompted agents over-purchased, overused filters, and failed to reproduce iterative search and revision.
architecture_implications: Train and evaluate on ordered real actions and outcomes rather than plausible narrated intentions.
Model iterative correction and non-purchase as normal behavior, not noise around a purchase objective.
Keep generated rationales separate from observed actions because synthetic reasoning is not evidence of a user's actual cognition.
limitations: The domain is online shopping with a simplified action space; textualized HTML omits visual and interaction cues, exact next-action scoring is strict, synthetic reasoning traces are not human explanations, and results do not generalize directly to personal life behavior.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org 2026.acl-long.2034 multi-turn human behavior customer data
ShopCART LLM agents real online customer behavior

title: MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
research_question: Can a language-model agent manage tiered memory through explicit paging operations to sustain conversations and reason over data beyond its context window?
method: Implemented an operating-system-inspired controller with main context, recall storage, archival storage, interrupts, and self-issued memory functions; evaluated multi-session conversation and deep document retrieval.
population_or_data: Multi-Session Chat personas and a generated sixth-session memory test, plus Deep Memory Retrieval and multi-document question-answering tasks.
core_finding: Self-managed paging substantially improved long-range retrieval, including Deep Memory Retrieval gains from 38.7 to 66.9 for GPT-3.5 and roughly 92-93 for tested GPT-4 variants, but failures remained when retrieval was noisy or the model stopped paging too early.
architecture_implications: Separate a small working context from durable recall and archival stores, with explicit read and write operations.
Let the controller iterate retrieval when evidence is insufficient, but enforce budgets and termination checks.
Persist raw dialogue separately from editable semantic memory so summaries can be revised without erasing evidence.
limitations: Results depend on proprietary models' function-calling behavior, several tasks and judges are model-generated, conversation personas are short and synthetic, embedding retrieval introduces noise, and the preprint does not establish fidelity to a real user.

search_queries: site:arxiv.org/abs 2310.08560 MemGPT operating systems memory
MemGPT deep memory retrieval multi-session chat

title: MemoryBank: Enhancing Large Language Models with Long-Term Memory
research_question: Can a memory bank with hierarchical summaries, user portraits, retrieval, and forgetting improve long-term personalized conversation?
method: Built MemoryBank and the SiliconFriend dialogue system with detailed records, multi-level summaries, a user portrait, vector retrieval, and an Ebbinghaus-inspired decay and reinforcement rule; evaluated simulated multi-day conversations and qualitative real-world cases.
population_or_data: Fifteen virtual users over ten days, 450 generated topics and 194 memory probes, plus a small set of real-world dialogue demonstrations.
core_finding: The memory layer improved access to prior conversational information and supported personalized responses, but retrieval success did not guarantee factual correctness or coherent use; the proposed forgetting curve was presented as an exploratory simplification rather than a validated model of human forgetting.
architecture_implications: Keep episodic dialogue, hierarchical summaries, and a mutable user portrait as distinct layers with source links.
Reinforce a memory only after verified reuse, and make decay reversible rather than deleting the sole evidence.
Score retrieval and answer correctness separately because a retrieved memory can still be misapplied.
limitations: Most evaluation conversations are model-generated, only 15 virtual users are followed for ten days, the portrait is not externally validated, qualitative supportive-dialogue examples are not clinical evidence, and the forgetting function has no person-specific empirical calibration.

search_queries: site:ojs.aaai.org MemoryBank Enhancing Large Language Models with Long-Term Memory
10.1609/aaai.v38i17.29946 MemoryBank

title: Long Time No See! Open-Domain Conversation with Long-Term Persona Memory
research_question: Can explicit extraction, updating, and retrieval of user and bot persona memories improve consistency across separated dialogue sessions?
method: Created the DuLeMon long-term dialogue corpus and PLATO-LTM, which extracts, filters, deduplicates, updates, and retrieves separate user and bot persona memories before response generation; evaluated automatic metrics, self-chat, and human judgments.
population_or_data: 27,501 Chinese dialogues with about 449,000 utterances, including 24,500 self-persona and 3,001 both-persona dialogues collected from crowdworkers.
core_finding: Explicit memory management markedly improved long-term persona consistency in self-chat human evaluation, while removing the memory extractor sharply reduced consistency; fine-tuning did not uniformly improve all dialogue qualities.
architecture_implications: Maintain separate memories for the user and agent to prevent identity attribution errors.
Run extraction, contradiction checks, deduplication, and updates before adding a persona fact to durable memory.
Evaluate memory over multiple separated sessions, including corrections and facts that should not be retained.
limitations: The corpus is Chinese and crowdworker-authored, long-horizon evaluation relies partly on a user simulator, memories are explicit persona facts rather than rich episodes, and the design leaves correction provenance, conflict policy, and capacity limits underexplored.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org 2022.findings-acl.207 Long Time No See persona memory
PLATO-LTM DuLeMon long-term persona memory

title: Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents
research_question: How well do language-model agents answer factual, temporal, commonsense, and adversarial questions over very long multi-session conversations?
method: Created LoCoMo with long temporally structured conversations, multimodal references, and question-answer tasks; compared long-context and retrieval-augmented methods with human performance.
population_or_data: Ten LLM-generated and human-edited conversations averaging 588 turns, 27.2 sessions, and 16,618 tokens, with 1,986 question-answer pairs and associated images.
core_finding: Humans reached 87.9 F1 while the best reported GPT-4 Turbo setup reached 51.6; long context and retrieval improved scores but left a large gap, especially for temporal and adversarial questions.
architecture_implications: Index events with explicit temporal relations and retrieve evidence across sessions rather than relying on one flat summary.
Add adversarial and unanswerable probes so the memory layer must distinguish absence from a plausible guess.
Evaluate memory retrieval, temporal reasoning, and final answer generation as separate stages.
limitations: Only ten conversations are included, and they are LLM-generated then partly human-edited rather than authentic personal histories; images come from the web rather than one person's visual life, and benchmark QA does not test consent, deletion, or live update behavior.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org 2024.acl-long.747 LoCoMo conversational memory
Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents

title: LongMemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term Interactive Memory
research_question: Can chat assistants reliably extract, integrate, update, temporally locate, and abstain over information accumulated across hundreds of sessions?
method: Built 500 expert-curated questions over synthetic long-term interaction histories and evaluated long-context and retrieval pipelines across five memory abilities, with ablations for indexing and retrieval design.
population_or_data: Histories around 115,000 tokens in the standard setting and stress tests up to 500 sessions or 1.5 million tokens, combining adapted conversations and simulated evidence sessions.
core_finding: Current systems suffered reported accuracy drops of roughly 30-60% over sustained histories; finer round-level indexing, fact-oriented key expansion, time-aware queries, and structured evidence notes improved results but did not eliminate update and abstention failures.
architecture_implications: Index at conversational-round granularity while retaining session boundaries and timestamps.
Store supersession links so later corrections override prior values without erasing historical state.
Require evidence notes and an abstention path when retrieved material is missing or conflicting.
limitations: Evidence sessions are constructed rather than naturally accumulated, histories mix adapted public conversations and simulation, many questions resemble needles in a controlled haystack, evaluation uses an LLM judge, and ownership, deletion, and social context are outside scope.

search_queries: site:proceedings.iclr.cc LongMemEval Benchmarking Chat Assistants Long-Term Interactive Memory
site:arxiv.org/abs 2410.10813 LongMemEval

title: SeCom: On Memory Construction and Retrieval for Personalized Conversational Agents
research_question: Which memory-unit granularity and denoising strategy best support retrieval-augmented responses over long conversations?
method: Compared turn-, session-, and summary-level memory; proposed SeCom to segment conversations into topical units and compress them before BM25 or dense retrieval; evaluated on LoCoMo, a reconstructed Long-MT-Bench+, and dialogue-segmentation datasets with ablations and human evaluation.
population_or_data: LoCoMo long conversations, constructed Long-MT-Bench+ conversations averaging 65.45 turns and 19,287 tokens, and DialSeg711, TIAGE, and SuperDialSeg segmentation data.
core_finding: Topical segment memory balanced fragmented turns against noisy sessions and lossy summaries; SeCom reached 71.57 GPT4Score on LoCoMo with BM25 versus 65.58 for turn-level BM25, and removing compression reduced the MPNet variant by up to 9.46 points.
architecture_implications: Construct memory units around coherent topic segments rather than fixed turns or entire sessions.
Denoise a retrieval representation while retaining the uncompressed source segment for evidence and correction.
Benchmark multiple sparse and dense retrievers because granularity interacts strongly with retrieval model choice.
limitations: Long-MT-Bench+ merges sessions and uses GPT-4-generated questions, early LoCoMo evaluation also used generated QA, GPT-4 supplies segmentation and much scoring, lighter segmenters lose accuracy, and the study does not test natural personal update, deletion, or contradiction workflows.

search_queries: site:proceedings.iclr.cc SeCom On Memory Construction and Retrieval for Personalized Conversational Agents
site:arxiv.org/abs 2502.05589 SeCom memory retrieval

title: When Personalization Legitimizes Risks: Uncovering Safety Vulnerabilities in Personalized Dialogue Agents
research_question: Can benign long-term memories and persona context weaken safety behavior in personalized dialogue agents, and can an inference-time intervention mitigate the effect?
method: Introduced PS-Bench, evaluated five memory-agent frameworks with five base models on matched stateless and personalized harmful queries, analyzed internal representations, and tested a detection-reflection intervention.
population_or_data: 272 multi-turn conversations, 750 base harmful queries, 1,986 additional persona-grounded or theme-augmented queries, eight safety categories, and synthetic-PII probes.
core_finding: Benign personalization increased attack success rates by 15.8% to 243.7% relative to stateless baselines; semantically aligned memories and explicit persona information could legitimize harmful intent, while a detection-reflection step reduced much of the degradation.
architecture_implications: Run safety classification after memory retrieval so the guard sees the same personal context as the generator.
Apply PII-aware sanitization and access control before personal memories enter a prompt.
After retrieval, detect memories that may legitimize a harmful query and inject a safety reflection before generation; monitor personalization utility separately.
limitations: PS-Bench partly synthesizes dialogue histories and harmful queries, covers only text and isolated harmful requests despite multi-session memories, samples but does not exhaust memory designs, uses an automatic detector with limited human validation, and does not test multimodal or tool-using agents.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org 2026.acl-long.1260 personalization legitimizes risks
PS-Bench intent legitimation personalized dialogue agents
